text: A sixty-year-old female with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and acid reflux noted several new episodes of shortness of breath that woke her from sleep. A chest x-ray was obtained which demonstrated an elevated right hemidiaphragm with increased opacity in the right lower lung field concerning for a diaphragmatic hernia. MRI of the chest identified a large morgagni hernia of the right hemidiaphragm with extensive herniation of fat, small and large bowel to the level of the aortic arch with a four point six centimeter defect.

In the operating room, a varus needle is inserted into the umbilicus to establish pneumoperitoneum, followed by the placement of a 10mm trocar below the xiphoid to the left of midline. Under direct visualization, five additional trocars are placed: 5mm and 12mm trocars in the left upper quadrant, a 5mm port in the subxiphoid space, and 5mm and 12mm trocars in the right upper quadrant. Upon exploration, a retrosternal diaphragmatic defect is observed with herniation of the entire small bowel, omentum, and transverse colon. The hernia is incarcerated and cannot be reduced initially. Dissection begins with blunt traction on the right of the defect, dividing adhesions and removing the falciform ligament with traction and ligature. The anterior and posterior portions of the hernia sac are mobilized, leading to the complete reduction of the hernia contents.

The surgeon measures the diaphragmatic defect at approximately ten by six centimeters. A Jackson Pratt drain is placed in the right lower thoracic cavity and exits through the five millimeter subxiphoid port. The surrounding tissue is cleaned of debris, and a ten centimeter by fifteen centimeter polyester parietex composite mesh is sutured circumferentially with number one proline to repair the defect.

Post-operative CT scans demonstrated satisfactory reduction of the hernia as well as fully expanded lung fields. Morgagni hernias occur due to a diaphragmatic defect in the sternocostal hiatus often due to failure of pars sternalis closure. Most are discovered acutely in pediatric patients due to the recurrent chest infections and pulmonary symptoms. Resentations in the adult population generally occurs later because of their overall asymptomatic nature. Rarely, adult patients may develop acute surgical conditions such as gastric volvulus, bowel obstruction, strangulation, and respiratory failure. These hernias are typically found incidentally on chest x-ray, but CT with oral contrast remains the imaging of choice. Both symptomatic and asymptomatic patients are recommended to undergo surgical repair which may be approached laparoscopically or open. In the acute surgical abdomen, an open approach may be preferred whereas elective repairs can be attempted laparoscopically.
